Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I'd usually try to study when I felt like it, which wasn't that often given my low motivation. I have access to good resources and tech, so I'd often tell myself I could catch up anytime, but then I'd get distracted with extracurriculars or just lose focus. I learn best by listening, so I'd put on lecture videos while doing other things, but I'm not sure how much actually stuck.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly driven by whatever deadline was coming up next. I didn't have a set schedule at all - some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram. With my extracurricular activities taking up time, I'd often push studying to whenever I had gaps, which meant late nights or rushed sessions. Regular study times just never became a thing for me.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about something due would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was missing. Occasionally I'd go on because I saw classmates talking about an assignment and realized I was behind. It wasn't really about wanting to learn - more about not wanting to completely fail.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I'd log in when I needed to, but it never felt automatic or routine. Maybe early in the semester I tried to make it a habit, but that faded quickly. With only about 23% attendance and my motivation being what it is, the LMS just became this thing I'd visit when I absolutely had to rather than something integrated into my daily life.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched quite a lot of video content - it's easier for me to absorb information by listening than reading. The discussion boards were okay when people actually responded to questions. I found that hearing explanations multiple times helped things click better than just reading through slides.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and text-heavy resources. They just didn't work with how I learn, so I'd skim them at best or skip them entirely. Some of the optional practice activities I'd put off indefinitely because they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up. I also didn't engage much with peer discussions beyond what felt necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. If it was going to help me pass, I'd do it. Also, if it was audio or video-based, I was more likely to engage because that's how I learn best. Anything that felt like busy work or wasn't clearly connected to my grade got pushed aside pretty quickly.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones at least had some urgency attached - there'd be consequences if I didn't do them. Optional stuff just fell to the bottom of my priority list every single time. I kept telling myself I'd get to them eventually, but with my motivation already low, 'eventually' never came. The lack of immediate consequences made them easy to ignore.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing other stuff. I also used some external YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials sometimes. My friends and I would have study sessions where we'd quiz each other verbally, which works great for how I learn. None of that would show up in the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd be going over concepts with friends in person, just talking through problems and explaining things to each other. I'd also replay downloaded audio while exercising or before bed. Sometimes I'd be genuinely processing course material in my head while doing other activities, working through concepts mentally. The LMS would just show I logged in once to download something.
Q11: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd download the video lectures and then watch them offline multiple times. Once I had the files, I didn't need to go back to the LMS. I'd listen to the same explanations over and over until they made sense. This was actually my main study method, but it would only register as one download or one view in the system.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I started feeling surprised by how quickly I was falling behind despite having all these resources available. My resilience isn't great, so when things got hard, I kind of shut down instead of pushing through. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit with my stress, but by then I'd already missed a lot. Extracurricular commitments also competed for my attention.
Q13: Yeah, definitely. When I was tired from activities or just mentally drained, the LMS felt overwhelming. I'd log in, see all the things I needed to do, and just close it. Confusion about what to prioritize made me freeze up sometimes. I'd tell myself I'd figure it out later, but later kept getting pushed back. The workload felt manageable on paper but not in practice.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd be logged in, maybe with a video playing, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. I'd be thinking about other things or just too tired to focus. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. It looked like engagement but my brain was elsewhere. Those sessions were basically wasted time.
Q15: All the time. I'd avoid starting assignments because they felt overwhelming, even when I knew waiting would make things worse. The bigger the task, the more I'd procrastinate. I think my low motivation played a huge role - I just couldn't make myself care enough to start early. I'd tell myself I work better under pressure anyway, which wasn't really true.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally kick into gear, cramming everything at the last minute. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It wasn't healthy or effective, but that pressure was the only motivation that actually worked. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done anything.
Q17: Feedback came pretty slowly for me - my average response time was over 22 hours. When I did get feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, but the effect wore off fast. If feedback was critical, I'd feel discouraged rather than motivated to improve. Positive feedback was nice but didn't really change my behavior much. I needed more immediate responses to stay engaged.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really push me to stay consistent. With my low attendance, I missed a lot of the rhythm the course was supposed to have. The flexibility that should have helped me actually made it easier to fall behind because there was no one checking on me regularly. I needed more structure than what was provided, honestly.
Q19: The video content was great for my learning style, so that helped. But the heavy reliance on text-based materials and readings made engagement harder for me. Also, the course felt a bit disconnected - I couldn't always see how pieces fit together. More audio content or podcasts would have helped me a lot. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than natural.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me - logging in, watching videos, browsing around, but not actually completing the work. You might be exploring or trying to understand but not having the motivation or ability to turn that into finished assignments. Or you could be overwhelmed and just clicking through things without really engaging. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That makes sense to me. If someone learns well offline or has good external resources, they might not need the LMS much. I did a lot of learning through downloaded content and conversations with friends that wouldn't show up. Someone could be genuinely understanding the material through other means. The LMS is just one tool, not the whole picture of learning.
Q22: The log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, how many videos you started. But it can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. It misses offline study completely, group discussions, mental processing of concepts. It also can't capture motivation or emotional state. Someone could have high activity but zero comprehension, or vice versa.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and actively trying to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things - it's being mentally present and curious. For me, it would mean listening to lectures and actually thinking about the concepts, asking questions, connecting ideas. I wasn't really engaged this semester, if I'm honest.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with motivation or how my extracurriculars competed for my time and energy. It wouldn't capture the audio learning I did offline or the study conversations with friends. It also wouldn't show that I was genuinely surprised by my performance - I thought I was doing okay until I wasn't. My internal experience was very different from what the data probably suggests.
